title: Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infection (CAUTI) in a 72-Year-Old Post-Op Patient with a Lingering Indwelling Catheter: Recognizing Symptomatic CAUTI (Including New Confusion) vs ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA, the CAUTI PREVENTION BUNDLE and Early Catheter Removal, Proper Specimen Collection, Treatment, and Preventing Progression to Urosepsis
subtitle: A CATHETER-ASSOCIATED URINARY TRACT INFECTION (CAUTI) is a urinary tract infection occurring in a patient with an indwelling urinary (Foley) catheter or one recently removed; it is the MOST COMMON healthcare-associated infection. The RISK rises with EACH DAY the catheter remains in place, because the catheter provides a direct route for organisms and supports BIOFILM formation on its surface, leading first to bacteriuria and then to infection. RECOGNITION: in a catheterized patient the classic UTI symptoms of dysuria, frequency, and urgency are often ABSENT (the catheter bypasses the urethra), so the nurse watches for NEW FEVER, SUPRAPUBIC tenderness/pain, COSTOVERTEBRAL-ANGLE (flank) tenderness, NEW-ONSET or WORSENING CONFUSION/DELIRIUM (in OLDER ADULTS this may be the ONLY sign), rigors, hematuria, and laboratory leukocytosis; urinalysis may show pyuria, bacteriuria, positive leukocyte esterase, and nitrites. A CRITICAL distinction is SYMPTOMATIC CAUTI versus ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA (ASB) — a POSITIVE urine culture WITHOUT signs/symptoms of infection. ASB is generally NOT TREATED with antibiotics (key exceptions: pregnancy and before certain urologic procedures), because treating it drives ANTIBIOTIC RESISTANCE and C. difficile risk without benefit; likewise, CLOUDY or FOUL-SMELLING urine ALONE, without symptoms, is NOT a reason to culture or treat. SPECIMEN: obtain the urine culture ASEPTICALLY from the catheter SAMPLING PORT (after cleansing it) — NOT from the drainage bag; if the catheter has been in place a long time (e.g., >2 weeks) and infection is suspected, replace it and collect from the fresh catheter. The CAUTI PREVENTION BUNDLE is the heart of nursing care: (1) AVOID unnecessary catheters — insert ONLY for an APPROPRIATE INDICATION (acute urinary retention or obstruction; need for accurate output in the critically ill; selected perioperative use; healing of open sacral/perineal wounds in incontinent patients; prolonged immobilization; comfort at end of life) and NOT for convenience, routine incontinence, or simply to obtain urine from a cooperative patient; (2) ASEPTIC/STERILE insertion by trained personnel (sterile gloves, drape, antiseptic, single-use lubricant); (3) maintain a CLOSED drainage system (do not disconnect; if the system is breached, replace it aseptically); (4) SECURE the catheter to prevent movement/urethral traction and keep urine flowing freely (no kinks/dependent loops); (5) keep the drainage BAG BELOW THE LEVEL OF THE BLADDER at ALL times (including transport/ambulation) and off the floor, and empty it regularly into a clean, separate container; (6) MEATAL/PERINEAL HYGIENE with soap and water (routine antiseptic ointments are not recommended); (7) HAND HYGIENE before and after any catheter manipulation and gloves for handling; (8) perform a DAILY REVIEW of CONTINUED NECESSITY and REMOVE the catheter as EARLY as possible — early removal is the SINGLE MOST EFFECTIVE intervention, supported by nurse-driven removal protocols and reminders; (9) do NOT perform routine bladder irrigation, routine catheter changes, or give prophylactic antibiotics to prevent CAUTI; and (10) use ALTERNATIVES when possible (intermittent straight catheterization, EXTERNAL/condom catheters, a bladder scanner for retention, scheduled voiding/toileting). TREATMENT of a SYMPTOMATIC CAUTI: obtain the culture, REMOVE the catheter if it is no longer indicated, or REPLACE it (to eliminate biofilm) if it must remain, then give APPROPRIATE antibiotics (empiric, then narrowed per culture and sensitivities) with hydration; practice ANTIBIOTIC STEWARDSHIP (treat only true symptomatic infection and tailor therapy). The nurse MONITORS for progression to UROSEPSIS — fever, tachycardia, HYPOTENSION, rising lactate/WBC, and altered mentation — which is a sepsis emergency requiring rapid cultures, antibiotics, and fluids. NURSING ROLE: prevent CAUTI through the bundle and early removal; distinguish symptomatic CAUTI from asymptomatic bacteriuria; collect the specimen correctly; treat symptomatic infection and remove/replace the catheter; monitor for and prevent urosepsis; advocate for daily necessity review; and educate the patient and team.

patient.name: Walter K.
patient.code: Full code; goal = recognize and treat a symptomatic catheter-associated UTI, remove/replace the indwelling catheter, prevent progression to urosepsis, and apply the CAUTI prevention bundle with early catheter removal
patient.admitDate: A 72-year-old man is post-operative day 4 after an open abdominal (sigmoid colectomy) procedure. An indwelling urinary (Foley) catheter placed in the operating room is still in place, although he is now hemodynamically stable, ambulating, and no longer requires strict output monitoring. Today he develops a new fever to 38.5°C, suprapubic discomfort, cloudy and malodorous urine, and new-onset CONFUSION noted by his nurse and family. He has no dysuria (the catheter bypasses the urethra). The clinical picture suggests a symptomatic CATHETER-ASSOCIATED UTI; the nurse must recognize it (vs asymptomatic bacteriuria), collect a proper specimen, address the catheter, support treatment, monitor for urosepsis, and reinforce the prevention bundle.
patient.room: Surgical med-surg unit; indwelling urinary catheter in place since OR (day 4); interdisciplinary team (surgery/hospitalist, bedside RN, infection prevention, pharmacy/antibiotic stewardship); evaluating for symptomatic CAUTI vs asymptomatic bacteriuria, catheter removal/replacement, treatment, and prevention

vitals.temp: 38.5°C; NEW fever with suprapubic discomfort, CLOUDY/malodorous urine, and NEW-ONSET CONFUSION (which may be the only sign in an older adult); indwelling catheter in place since the OR (day 4) with no current clear indication — picture suggests a symptomatic CATHETER-ASSOCIATED UTI

38.4°C; urine culture obtained ASEPTICALLY from the catheter SAMPLING PORT (NOT the drainage bag); urinalysis shows pyuria, bacteriuria, positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites, with leukocytosis on CBC — symptomatic CAUTI (distinguished from asymptomatic bacteriuria, which would lack symptoms)

38.9°C; WARNING pattern for progression to UROSEPSIS — rising fever, tachycardia, falling blood pressure, increasing confusion, and a rising lactate/WBC; this is a sepsis emergency requiring rapid cultures, antibiotics, and fluid resuscitation

38.2°C; the indwelling catheter is REMOVED (no longer indicated) — or REPLACED to remove biofilm if it must remain; APPROPRIATE antibiotics started after the culture (empiric, then narrowed per sensitivities) with IV fluids/hydration; output and response monitored

37.6°C; CAUTI prevention bundle reinforced unit-wide and for this patient: closed drainage system, bag BELOW the bladder and off the floor, secured catheter with free flow, perineal care, hand hygiene, DAILY necessity review with early removal, and alternatives (intermittent/external catheter, bladder scanner) — no routine irrigation/changes/prophylactic antibiotics
37.0°C; afebrile, mental status cleared, voiding normally without the catheter; infection resolving on culture-directed antibiotics; educated on prevention and discharged catheter-free

labs.n: PRESENTATION + recognizing CAUTI (the most common HAI; signs; confusion in the older adult)
labs.v: Walter K. is post-operative day 4 after an open sigmoid colectomy, with an indwelling urinary (Foley) catheter placed in the OR that REMAINS in place even though he is now stable, ambulating, and no longer needs strict output monitoring. He develops a NEW fever (38.5°C), SUPRAPUBIC discomfort, CLOUDY/malodorous urine, and NEW-ONSET CONFUSION. This suggests a CATHETER-ASSOCIATED UTI (CAUTI) — a UTI in a patient with an indwelling catheter (or recently removed), and the MOST COMMON healthcare-associated infection. The catheter provides a direct route for organisms and supports BIOFILM formation, and the RISK rises with EACH catheter-day. In a catheterized patient, the classic dysuria/frequency/urgency are often ABSENT (the catheter bypasses the urethra), so recognition relies on NEW FEVER, SUPRAPUBIC pain, COSTOVERTEBRAL-ANGLE (flank) tenderness, NEW or WORSENING CONFUSION/DELIRIUM — which in OLDER ADULTS may be the ONLY sign — rigors, hematuria, and leukocytosis. The nurse's priorities: recognize symptomatic CAUTI (and distinguish it from asymptomatic bacteriuria), collect a proper specimen, address the lingering catheter, support treatment, and watch for urosepsis.
labs.r: CAUTI = UTI with an indwelling (or recently removed) catheter — the MOST COMMON HAI; risk rises with EACH catheter-day (biofilm). Classic dysuria/frequency often ABSENT in catheterized patients → watch for NEW fever, suprapubic/CVA (flank) tenderness, NEW CONFUSION (may be the ONLY sign in elders), hematuria, leukocytosis. RN role: recognize symptomatic CAUTI in a catheterized patient.

labs.n: Symptomatic CAUTI vs ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA + proper specimen collection
labs.v: A pivotal distinction is SYMPTOMATIC CAUTI versus ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA (ASB). ASB is a POSITIVE urine culture WITHOUT signs or symptoms of infection; it is COMMON in catheterized patients and is generally NOT TREATED with antibiotics — the important EXCEPTIONS are PREGNANCY and BEFORE certain urologic procedures. Treating ASB provides NO benefit and drives ANTIBIOTIC RESISTANCE and C. difficile infection; therefore CLOUDY or FOUL-SMELLING urine ALONE, without symptoms, is NOT a reason to send a culture or start antibiotics (a key antibiotic-stewardship point and a common test trap). A symptomatic CAUTI, by contrast, has the systemic/local SIGNS above plus laboratory evidence. SPECIMEN COLLECTION matters: obtain the urine culture ASEPTICALLY from the catheter's SAMPLING PORT after cleansing it — NEVER from the DRAINAGE BAG (where organisms multiply and contaminate the sample) and never by disconnecting the closed system. If the catheter has been in place a long time (e.g., >2 weeks) and infection is suspected, the catheter should be REPLACED and the specimen collected from the FRESH catheter so the culture reflects bladder urine rather than catheter biofilm. Urinalysis findings (pyuria, bacteriuria, leukocyte esterase, nitrites) support but do not alone diagnose infection without symptoms.
labs.r: ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA (positive culture, NO symptoms) → do NOT treat (exceptions: pregnancy + before urologic procedures); treating it = resistance + C. diff with no benefit. Cloudy/foul urine ALONE ≠ CAUTI. Symptomatic CAUTI = symptoms + lab evidence. Specimen: aseptic from the SAMPLING PORT (NOT the bag); if catheter old (>2 wk), replace then collect. RN role: distinguish symptomatic CAUTI from ASB + collect the specimen correctly.

labs.n: CAUTI PREVENTION BUNDLE + early catheter removal (the core of nursing care)
labs.v: Most CAUTIs are PREVENTABLE, and the bundle is core nursing care: (1) AVOID unnecessary catheters — place ONLY for an APPROPRIATE INDICATION (acute urinary RETENTION or obstruction; accurate output in the CRITICALLY ill; selected PERIOPERATIVE use; open SACRAL/PERINEAL wounds in incontinent patients; prolonged immobilization; end-of-life COMFORT) and NOT for convenience, routine incontinence, or simply to obtain a specimen from a cooperative patient. (2) ASEPTIC/sterile INSERTION by trained staff (sterile gloves, drape, antiseptic, single-use lubricant). (3) Maintain a CLOSED drainage system — do not disconnect; replace aseptically if breached. (4) SECURE the catheter to prevent traction/movement and keep urine flowing freely (no kinks/dependent loops). (5) Keep the drainage BAG BELOW the level of the BLADDER at ALL times — including during transport and ambulation — and OFF the floor, emptying it regularly into a clean, separate container (avoid touching the spout to the container). (6) MEATAL/PERINEAL HYGIENE with soap and water (routine antiseptic ointments are not recommended). (7) HAND HYGIENE before and after any catheter manipulation; gloves for handling. (8) Perform a DAILY REVIEW of continued NECESSITY and REMOVE the catheter as EARLY as possible — EARLY REMOVAL is the SINGLE MOST EFFECTIVE intervention (nurse-driven removal protocols and reminders help). (9) Do NOT perform routine bladder irrigation or routine catheter changes, and do NOT give prophylactic antibiotics. (10) Use ALTERNATIVES — intermittent straight catheterization, EXTERNAL/condom catheters, a BLADDER SCANNER for suspected retention, and scheduled toileting. For Walter, the catheter has lingered without a current indication, so the nurse advocates for prompt removal.
labs.r: CAUTI PREVENTION BUNDLE: insert ONLY for appropriate indications (retention/obstruction, critical-illness output, selected perioperative, sacral wounds + incontinence, immobilization, comfort) — NOT convenience/incontinence; ASEPTIC insertion; CLOSED system; SECURE + free flow; BAG BELOW BLADDER + off floor; perineal care; HAND HYGIENE; DAILY necessity review + EARLY REMOVAL (most effective); NO routine irrigation/changes/prophylactic abx; use ALTERNATIVES (intermittent/external cath, bladder scanner). RN role: prevent CAUTI + advocate early removal.

labs.n: Treatment of symptomatic CAUTI + progression to urosepsis + stewardship
labs.v: When a SYMPTOMATIC CAUTI is diagnosed: obtain the urine culture; then REMOVE the catheter if it is no longer indicated, or REPLACE it (to eliminate the infected BIOFILM) if it must remain; and start APPROPRIATE antibiotics — empiric coverage initially, then NARROWED per culture and sensitivities — with hydration. ANTIBIOTIC STEWARDSHIP is essential: treat only a TRUE symptomatic infection (not ASB), choose the right drug/dose/duration, and de-escalate per results to limit resistance and C. difficile. The nurse MONITORS for progression to UROSEPSIS — the spread of infection to the bloodstream causing sepsis — signaled by rising fever (or hypothermia), TACHYCARDIA, HYPOTENSION, tachypnea, worsening CONFUSION, decreased urine output, and a rising LACTATE/WBC. Urosepsis is a SEPSIS EMERGENCY requiring rapid blood and urine CULTURES, BROAD-SPECTRUM antibiotics within the first hour, IV FLUID resuscitation, and close hemodynamic monitoring (escalating to vasopressors/ICU if shock develops). Removing or replacing the SOURCE (the colonized catheter) is part of source control. For Walter, the team obtains the culture, removes the lingering catheter, starts antibiotics and fluids, and watches closely for any sign of urosepsis.
labs.r: Symptomatic CAUTI treatment: culture → REMOVE catheter (if not indicated) or REPLACE it (clear biofilm) → APPROPRIATE antibiotics (empiric then narrowed) + hydration; STEWARDSHIP (treat only true infection, de-escalate). MONITOR for UROSEPSIS (fever, tachycardia, HYPOTENSION, worsening confusion, rising lactate/WBC) → sepsis emergency: rapid cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h + IV fluids + source control (remove/replace catheter). RN role: treat the infection + prevent/recognize urosepsis.

labs.n: Nursing role + education; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: ONGOING NURSING CARE + EDUCATION: maintain the prevention bundle for every catheterized patient; assess catheter NECESSITY daily and advocate for the earliest safe REMOVAL; perform proper perineal care and hand hygiene; keep the closed system intact with the bag dependent; monitor intake/output, temperature, and mental status; collect specimens correctly; administer culture-directed antibiotics and ensure adequate hydration; and watch for urosepsis. EDUCATE the patient/family on the reason for (and prompt removal of) the catheter, perineal hygiene, keeping the bag below the bladder, hand hygiene, and reporting fever, suprapubic/flank pain, or confusion. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT treat asymptomatic bacteriuria or culture/treat based on cloudy/foul urine ALONE; did NOT collect the specimen from the drainage bag; did NOT leave the catheter in for convenience or perform routine irrigation/changes/prophylactic antibiotics; did NOT raise the drainage bag above the bladder; and did NOT delay recognizing and treating the symptomatic infection or its progression to urosepsis. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: prompt recognition of symptomatic CAUTI; correct specimen collection; early catheter removal/replacement and source control; culture-directed, stewardship-minded antibiotics; diligent prevention bundle; and patient education. EXPECTED outcome: the symptomatic CAUTI resolves on appropriate therapy, the catheter is removed, urosepsis is prevented (or rapidly treated), and prevention practices reduce future infections. NURSING ROLE: prevent CAUTI through the bundle and early removal; distinguish symptomatic CAUTI from asymptomatic bacteriuria; collect the specimen correctly; treat symptomatic infection and remove/replace the catheter; monitor for and prevent urosepsis; and educate.
labs.r: ONGOING: maintain prevention bundle, DAILY necessity review + early removal, perineal care/hand hygiene, closed dependent system, monitor I&O/temp/mentation, culture-directed antibiotics + hydration, watch urosepsis; EDUCATE (catheter purpose/removal, hygiene, bag below bladder, report fever/flank pain/confusion). NEGATIVES (correct practice): no treating ASB/cloudy urine alone, no specimen from the bag, no convenience catheter/routine irrigation/prophylactic abx, no raising the bag, no delayed treatment. RN role: prevent + recognize + treat + educate.

nursesNote: Surgical med-surg unit — Walter K., 72, POD4 open sigmoid colectomy; OR-placed indwelling Foley STILL in place though stable, ambulating, no longer needing strict output monitoring. NEW fever 38.5°C, suprapubic discomfort, CLOUDY/malodorous urine, NEW-ONSET CONFUSION (no dysuria — catheter bypasses urethra) → symptomatic CATHETER-ASSOCIATED UTI (CAUTI = most common HAI; risk rises each catheter-day; biofilm). In catheterized patients classic dysuria/frequency often ABSENT → recognize via NEW fever, suprapubic/CVA tenderness, NEW CONFUSION (may be ONLY sign in elders), hematuria, leukocytosis. KEY distinction: symptomatic CAUTI vs ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA (positive culture, NO symptoms → do NOT treat except pregnancy/pre-urologic procedure; treating drives resistance + C. diff); cloudy/foul urine ALONE ≠ CAUTI. SPECIMEN: aseptic from the catheter SAMPLING PORT (NOT the drainage bag); if catheter old (>2wk), replace then collect. UA: pyuria/bacteriuria/leukocyte esterase/nitrites + leukocytosis. PREVENTION BUNDLE (core nursing): insert ONLY for appropriate indication (retention/obstruction, critical-illness output, selected perioperative, sacral wounds + incontinence, immobilization, comfort) — NOT convenience/incontinence; ASEPTIC insertion; CLOSED system; SECURE + free flow (no kinks/dependent loops); BAG BELOW BLADDER at all times incl. transport/ambulation + off floor; perineal care soap+water; HAND HYGIENE; DAILY necessity review + EARLIEST safe REMOVAL (single most effective); NO routine irrigation/changes/prophylactic antibiotics; ALTERNATIVES (intermittent straight cath, external/condom cath, bladder scanner, scheduled toileting). This catheter lingered without indication → advocated prompt REMOVAL. TREATMENT: culture → REMOVE (no longer indicated) or REPLACE (clear biofilm) catheter → APPROPRIATE antibiotics (empiric → narrowed per sensitivities) + hydration; STEWARDSHIP. MONITOR for UROSEPSIS (fever, tachycardia, HYPOTENSION 100/58, worsening confusion, rising lactate/WBC) → sepsis emergency: rapid cultures + broad-spectrum abx within 1h + IV fluids + source control. EDUCATE patient/family (catheter purpose/early removal, hygiene, bag below bladder, report fever/flank pain/confusion). NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT treat ASB/cloudy urine alone, did NOT collect from the bag, did NOT keep a convenience catheter or do routine irrigation/changes/prophylactic abx, did NOT raise the bag above the bladder, did NOT delay treatment. Afebrile + clear mentation + voiding catheter-free at recovery.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: surgical med-surg unit; POD4 sigmoid colectomy; evaluate symptomatic catheter-associated UTI
DIAGNOSIS: symptomatic CAUTI — monitor for progression to urosepsis; distinguish from asymptomatic bacteriuria
Obtain urine culture aseptically from the catheter sampling port (NOT the drainage bag); if catheter >2 weeks old and infection suspected, replace it and collect from the new catheter
Urinalysis, CBC, basic metabolic panel, lactate; blood cultures if signs of sepsis
Assess indication: if no longer indicated, REMOVE the indwelling catheter; if still required, REPLACE it to eliminate biofilm
Daily review of continued necessity; use a nurse-driven removal protocol; consider alternatives (intermittent straight catheterization, external/condom catheter, bladder scanner for retention)
Start appropriate empiric antibiotics after the culture; narrow per culture and sensitivities (antibiotic stewardship); provide IV fluids/hydration
Do NOT treat asymptomatic bacteriuria; do NOT culture/treat based on cloudy or foul-smelling urine alone without symptoms
Maintain a closed drainage system; secure the catheter; keep the drainage bag below the bladder at all times (including transport) and off the floor; ensure unobstructed flow
Perineal/meatal hygiene with soap and water; hand hygiene before/after any manipulation; no routine bladder irrigation, routine catheter changes, or prophylactic antibiotics
Vital signs, mental status, and intake/output; monitor for urosepsis (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, worsening confusion, rising lactate/WBC) and escalate rapidly if it develops
EDUCATION: catheter purpose and prompt removal, perineal hygiene, keeping the bag dependent, hand hygiene, and reporting fever, suprapubic/flank pain, or confusion

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this post-op patient with a lingering catheter?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: New fever, suprapubic discomfort, cloudy/malodorous urine, and new-onset confusion in a catheterized patient — a symptomatic catheter-associated UTI
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the symptomatic CAUTI picture. RN role: recognize symptomatic CAUTI.

opts.text: The indwelling catheter has been in place since surgery (day 4) with no current clear indication — each catheter-day increases CAUTI risk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the lingering catheter is the modifiable source; risk rises daily. RN role: assess catheter necessity.

opts.text: New-onset confusion/delirium may be the ONLY sign of a UTI/CAUTI in an older adult
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — atypical presentation (confusion) in elders is high-yield. RN role: recognize confusion as a possible infection sign.

opts.text: Obtain the urine culture aseptically from the catheter sampling port (not the drainage bag), with urinalysis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — correct specimen collection is essential for an accurate culture. RN role: collect the specimen properly.

opts.text: Monitor for progression to urosepsis — fever, tachycardia, hypotension, worsening confusion, rising lactate/WBC
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — urosepsis is the immediate life-threat to anticipate. RN role: monitor for sepsis.

opts.text: Review whether the catheter meets an appropriate indication; if not, advocate for prompt removal
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — early removal is the single most effective CAUTI intervention. RN role: advocate for early removal.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document before antibiotics
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant before antibiotic therapy. Not the central cue.

opts.text: A positive urine culture with NO symptoms (asymptomatic bacteriuria) should always be treated with antibiotics
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Asymptomatic bacteriuria is generally NOT treated (exceptions: pregnancy, pre-urologic procedures); treating it drives resistance and C. difficile without benefit.

opts.text: Cloudy or foul-smelling urine alone, without symptoms, is enough to diagnose and treat a CAUTI
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Appearance/odor alone does not equal infection; symptomatic CAUTI requires signs/symptoms plus laboratory evidence — culturing/treating on appearance alone promotes overtreatment.

opts.text: CAUTI is the most common healthcare-associated infection; risk rises with each catheter-day, and biofilm on the catheter promotes infection
opts.rat: CORRECT — epidemiology/pathophysiology. Foundation for prevention.

opts.text: In a catheterized patient, classic dysuria/frequency may be absent; fever, suprapubic/flank pain, and new confusion are key signs
opts.rat: CORRECT — atypical presentation in catheterized patients. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: Asymptomatic bacteriuria (positive culture without symptoms) should NOT be treated (except in pregnancy or before urologic procedures) because treating it promotes resistance
opts.rat: CORRECT — the asymptomatic-bacteriuria/stewardship principle. Foundation for avoiding overtreatment.

opts.text: The single most effective prevention is removing the catheter as early as possible, and catheters should be inserted only for appropriate indications
opts.rat: CORRECT — early removal + appropriate indications. Foundation for the prevention bundle.

opts.text: Maintaining a closed system, keeping the bag below the bladder, securing the catheter, aseptic insertion, hand hygiene, and perineal care prevent CAUTI
opts.rat: CORRECT — bundle elements. Foundation for prevention practice.

opts.text: If a symptomatic CAUTI is diagnosed and the catheter must remain, it should be replaced (to remove biofilm), with a culture obtained and appropriate antibiotics started
opts.rat: CORRECT — source control + treatment principle. Foundation for managing symptomatic infection.

opts.text: Routine bladder irrigation, routine catheter changes, and prophylactic antibiotics are recommended to prevent CAUTI
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. These are NOT recommended and do not prevent CAUTI; prevention relies on the bundle and early removal.

opts.text: Indwelling catheters should be placed for convenience or simple incontinence to reduce nursing workload
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Convenience and routine incontinence are NOT appropriate indications; unnecessary catheters increase CAUTI risk — use alternatives.

opts.text: Recognize the symptomatic CAUTI and assess for/monitor progression to urosepsis (vitals, mental status, lactate/WBC)
opts.rat: #1 — Potential urosepsis is the immediate life-threat to detect and anticipate. RN role: recognize infection + monitor for sepsis.

opts.text: Obtain a proper urine culture (aseptic, from the sampling port) and labs and notify the provider before antibiotics
opts.rat: #2 — Cultures before antibiotics guide accurate, stewardship-minded therapy. RN role: correct specimen + notify provider.

opts.text: Remove or replace the indwelling catheter (source control — remove if not indicated; replace to clear biofilm if still needed)
opts.rat: #3 — Source control of the colonized catheter is essential to resolution. RN role: address the catheter.

opts.text: Administer appropriate antibiotics and provide hydration per orders for the symptomatic infection
opts.rat: #4 — Treats the infection once cultures are obtained and the source addressed. RN role: antibiotics + hydration.

opts.text: Reinforce the CAUTI prevention bundle, advocate daily necessity review/early removal, and educate; document
opts.rat: #5 — Prevention and education sustain the outcome and protect future patients. RN role: prevention bundle + education.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: Assess for symptomatic CAUTI and monitor for urosepsis (vitals, mental status, lactate/WBC); notify the provider
opts.rat: Recognizes infection and anticipates sepsis. RN role: assess + monitor + escalate.

opts.text: Obtain the urine culture aseptically from the catheter sampling port (not the drainage bag) before starting antibiotics
opts.rat: Correct specimen technique for an accurate culture. RN role: proper specimen collection.

opts.text: Remove the catheter if it no longer meets an indication, or replace it (to remove biofilm) if it must remain
opts.rat: Source control of the colonized catheter. RN role: remove/replace the catheter.

opts.text: Start appropriate antibiotics and provide hydration per orders, narrowing therapy per culture results
opts.rat: Culture-directed, stewardship-minded treatment. RN role: antibiotics + hydration.

opts.text: Maintain a closed system, keep the bag below the bladder, secure the catheter, perform perineal care and hand hygiene, and review necessity daily
opts.rat: Core prevention bundle. RN role: prevent CAUTI + advocate early removal.

opts.text: Consider alternatives to indwelling catheterization (intermittent straight catheter, external/condom catheter, bladder scanner for retention)
opts.rat: Reduces catheter use and CAUTI risk. RN role: use alternatives when possible.

opts.text: Treat asymptomatic bacteriuria with antibiotics whenever a culture is positive
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — asymptomatic bacteriuria is generally not treated (exceptions: pregnancy, pre-urologic procedures); treating it drives resistance/C. diff.

opts.text: Diagnose and treat a CAUTI based on cloudy/foul-smelling urine alone, without symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — appearance/odor alone does not indicate infection; this leads to overtreatment. Require symptoms plus lab evidence.

opts.text: Perform routine bladder irrigation, routine catheter changes, and give prophylactic antibiotics to prevent CAUTI
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — none of these prevent CAUTI; prevention relies on the bundle and early removal.

opts.text: Keep the catheter in for convenience and raise the drainage bag above the bladder when moving the patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — convenience is not an indication, and raising the bag above the bladder causes reflux of contaminated urine, increasing infection risk.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the patient (vitals, mental status, abdomen/flank), recognize the symptomatic CAUTI and possible urosepsis, and notify the provider
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Recognition and assessment for sepsis drive urgency and the plan. RN role: assess + recognize + escalate.

opts.text: Obtain the urine culture aseptically from the sampling port (and blood cultures/labs if septic) before antibiotics
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Cultures before antibiotics ensure accurate, targeted therapy. RN role: collect specimens.

opts.text: Remove or replace the indwelling catheter per orders (source control)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Addressing the colonized catheter is essential to resolution. RN role: remove/replace the catheter.

opts.text: Administer appropriate antibiotics and IV fluids per orders and monitor the response
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Treats the infection and supports perfusion. RN role: antibiotics + fluids + monitoring.

opts.text: Reinforce the prevention bundle, advocate daily catheter-necessity review and early removal, educate, and document
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing. Prevention, advocacy, education, and documentation sustain the outcome. RN role: prevention + education.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After catheter removal, cultures, culture-directed antibiotics, and hydration, the fever resolves, mental status clears, and the urine clears
opts.rat: Source control plus appropriate therapy resolving the infection. Expected, desired response.

opts.text: The catheter was removed once it no longer met an indication, and the patient voided normally without recurrent infection
opts.rat: Early removal eliminates the source and risk. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: With the prevention bundle (closed system, bag below the bladder, perineal care, hand hygiene, daily necessity review), no new CAUTI develops
opts.rat: Effective prevention practice. Improving/safe trajectory.

opts.text: Antibiotics were narrowed to the cultured organism's sensitivities, and the symptomatic infection resolved
opts.rat: Culture-directed stewardship with resolution. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — asymptomatic bacteriuria was treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics; the patient then developed C. difficile and a resistant organism
opts.rat: ⚠️ Treating asymptomatic bacteriuria caused harm (C. diff, resistance). Demonstrates why ASB is not treated.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the catheter was kept for convenience with the bag resting on the floor and the closed system repeatedly opened; bacteriuria and then a symptomatic CAUTI developed
opts.rat: ⚠️ Bundle breaches (unnecessary catheter, contaminated bag, open system) caused infection. Demonstrates why the bundle and early removal matter.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the symptomatic CAUTI was not treated and the catheter was not addressed; the patient progressed to urosepsis with hypotension
opts.rat: ⚠️ Untreated infection without source control progressed to urosepsis. Demonstrates the need for timely treatment and monitoring.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the drainage bag was raised above the bladder during transport, causing reflux of contaminated urine and increasing infection risk
opts.rat: ⚠️ Raising the bag above the bladder causes reflux. Demonstrates keeping the bag dependent at all times.

opts.text: Early after starting treatment, before antibiotics take effect, the fever and confusion are still present and essentially unchanged while cultures are pending
opts.rat: Before antibiotics take effect, an unchanged baseline is expected; continue treatment, hydration, and monitoring and reassess.

opts.text: At follow-up the patient is afebrile, catheter-free, voiding normally, and able to describe prevention measures
opts.rat: Sustained recovery with effective education. Ideal longer-term outcome.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
